summary: Where should an engagement program start? This page ranks states by opportunity — where three things coincide: a high burden of the selected condition, members with the financial headroom to engage with a health program (we use how far the employer's wage goes against local prices as the proxy), and enough members for a program to matter.

It is a relative screen for comparing states, not a prediction about any individual — and 'more engageable' is not the same as 'more savings': research consistently shows the members most likely to engage are often the ones who need intervention least.

moves.headline: The opportunity index
moves.detail: opportunity = condition prevalence × cost-adjusted pay × log(members), normalized 0–100 across the states where members actually live. The log dampens volume so the ranking doesn't simply collapse to the biggest states. Prevalence is the twin-cohort rate for the selected condition (see Clinical cost drivers).

moves.headline: Cost-adjusted pay (the engageability proxy)
moves.detail: A constant anchor wage deflated by each state's cost of living (BEA Regional Price Parities) and compared to the local median income (ACS), with broadband access folded in. The anchor cancels in the ranking; it only makes the dollar values interpretable.

moves.headline: Dollars at stake
moves.detail: members × covered-adults ratio × condition prevalence × the cited per-person annual cost (see the Clinical cost drivers methodology for each cost basis). Context, not the ranking basis.

moves.headline: Why financial headroom, not affluence
moves.detail: Financial scarcity consumes the attention needed for non-urgent, preventive health; and voluntary program participation skews toward higher-SES, already-healthier employees. Both effects are why the index reads 'wage vs. local prices' rather than raw area income.

citations.label: SES inequalities in workplace health promotion (IPD meta-analysis, 2020)

limitations: A screening heuristic — engageability is a literature-informed prior, not a coefficient fitted to this employer's engagement data.
'More engageable' ≠ 'more savings' (healthy-participant bias): the most engageable members may need intervention least.
State-level and relative — never a per-person probability, and not a verdict that low-index geographies aren't worth serving.

sources_detail.dataset: BEA Regional Price Parities (state, All items)
